front: What is constructor overloading in C++?
back: Constructor overloading is a technique where a class can have multiple constructors with the same name but different parameter lists. This allows objects to be initialized in different ways, providing flexibility during object creation.

front: What is the primary purpose of a copy constructor in C++?
back: The primary purpose of a copy constructor is to initialize a new object as a copy of an existing object of the same class. It is used when an object is passed by value, returned by value, or explicitly copied.

front: What is the signature of a typical copy constructor?
back: A typical copy constructor has the signature: `ClassName(const ClassName &old_obj)`. It takes a constant reference to an object of the same class as its parameter.

front: When is the copy constructor automatically called by the compiler?
back: The copy constructor is automatically called in three main scenarios: 1) When an object is passed by value to a function. 2) When an object is returned by value from a function. 3) When an object is initialized using another object of the same class (e.g., `MyClass obj2 = obj1;`).

front: What is a default constructor and how does overloading relate to it?
back: A default constructor is a constructor that can be called without any arguments. If no constructors are defined, the compiler provides a default one. When you overload constructors (define your own), the compiler no longer provides the implicit default constructor unless you explicitly define one with no parameters or use `ClassName() = default;`.
